paper_title: Inferring late-Holocene climate in the Ecuadorian Andes using a chironomid-based temperature inference model

methods.method_name: Chironomid-Based Temperature Inference Model Development and Validation
methods.description: This method details the process of analyzing a chironomid calibration dataset, selecting relevant environmental variables, developing quantitative temperature inference models using Weighted Averaging (WA) and Bayesian approaches, and rigorously evaluating their predictive performance and potential biases using techniques like jackknifing and residual analysis.

steps.description: Remove explanatory variables with Variance Inflation Factors (VIFs) > 20.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter environmental variables based on VIF threshold
inputs: Calibration dataset with environmental variables and chironomid assemblages
outputs: Filtered set of explanatory environmental variables

steps.description: Perform Canonical Correspondence Analysis (CCA) using the filtered explanatory variables and chironomid assemblage data.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform CCA on chironomid and environmental data
outputs: CCA results (variance explained by variables/axes, eigenvalues)

steps.description: Analyze the variance explained by the explanatory variables and the first two CCA axes. Identify variables explaining most variance (e.g., MAT).
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze CCA output for variance explanation and variable importance

steps.description: Perform Detrended Canonical Correspondence Analysis (DCCA) constrained by Mean Annual Temperature (MAT).
outputs: DCCA results (assemblage shift in SD units)

steps.description: Analyze the distribution of chironomid taxa along the temperature gradient (MAT).
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze taxon distribution along temperature gradient
outputs: Description of taxon distribution patterns (e.g., abundance peaks, presence/absence ranges)

steps.description: Calculate weighted average (WA) and Bayesian optima and tolerance for each chironomid taxon based on the calibration data.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate taxon optima and tolerances using WA and Bayesian methods

steps.description: Develop a Weighted Averaging (inverse) temperature inference model using the calibration dataset.
outputs: A trained Weighted Averaging (inverse) inference model object

steps.description: Develop a Bayesian temperature inference model using the calibration dataset.
outputs: A trained Bayesian inference model object

steps.description: Evaluate the predictive performance of the Weighted Averaging (inverse) model using leave-one-out jackknifing. Calculate performance statistics: R^2 jack, RMSEP jack, mean bias jack, max bias jack.
steps.searchable_summary: Evaluate WA model performance using jackknifing
outputs: Performance statistics for the WA model (R^2 jack, RMSEP jack, mean bias jack, max bias jack)

steps.description: Evaluate the predictive performance of the Bayesian model using leave-one-out jackknifing. Calculate performance statistics: R^2 jack, RMSEP jack, mean bias jack, max bias jack.
steps.searchable_summary: Evaluate Bayesian model performance using jackknifing
outputs: Performance statistics for the Bayesian model (R^2 jack, RMSEP jack, mean bias jack, max bias jack)

steps.description: Compare the calculated performance statistics (R^2 jack, RMSEP jack, bias) between the Weighted Averaging (inverse) and Bayesian models.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare performance metrics of different inference models
outputs: A comparison of model performance metrics

steps.description: Analyze the residuals (difference between observed and predicted MAT) of the inference models to identify patterns of underestimation or overestimation across the temperature gradient.
inputs: Calibration dataset (observed MAT, predicted MAT from models)
outputs: Residual analysis results (e.g., plot of residuals vs observed/predicted values)

steps.description: Assess potential factors that may influence model performance and introduce bias, such as the uneven distribution of calibration sites along the temperature gradient, low chironomid head capsule counts in some samples, the presence and abundance of eurythermic taxa, and potential "edge effects" caused by gaps in the temperature gradient.
steps.searchable_summary: Assess factors affecting model performance and bias
inputs: Characteristics of the calibration dataset (e.g., site distribution, head capsule counts per sample)
Characteristics of chironomid taxa (e.g., tolerance of eurythermic taxa)
outputs: Identification and discussion of potential sources of error and bias in the inference models
